intro: The clearest difference between Backtrace and Sentry is lock-in — Backtrace has medium lock-in (Crash reports exportable), whereas Sentry has low lock-in (Low — open-source, self-hosted option, OTel-compatible). Beyond that, developer experience — Backtrace scores 4/5 against Sentry's 5/5. Backtrace fits mobile; Sentry fits error tracking and crash reporting for any platform. On the strengths side, Backtrace is cited for mobile and web apps in production, while Sentry is cited for error tracking and crash reporting for any platform. The honest trade-off: Backtrace is the wrong call for pre-launch, Simple projects; Sentry is the wrong call for full APM with infrastructure monitoring; Backtrace users flag Pre-launch/beta apps, and Sentry users flag full APM with infrastructure monitoring. Choosing between them should come down to the constraint that actually binds your workflow, not surface-level overlap. On developer experience the data shows Backtrace at 4/5 and Sentry at 5/5, with transparency at 4/5 and 4/5 respectively.
quick_take: Backtrace is for mobile; Sentry is for error tracking and crash reporting; decide based on lock-in tolerance.
verdict_a: Choose Backtrace if your use case is mobile, and medium lock-in is acceptable in exchange for its fit.
verdict_b: Choose Sentry if your use case is error tracking and crash reporting for any platform, and low lock-in is acceptable in exchange for its fit.

faq.a: Both Backtrace and Sentry use a freemium model with a free tier, so raw pricing is close on paper. The real cost difference depends on volume, seats, and which paid features you actually need. Check current pricing on each vendor's page before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Backtrace to Sentry?
faq.a: Migrating from Backtrace to Sentry is feasible when their scopes overlap, but the work is in re-mapping configuration, data models, and integrations. Moving off Backtrace is harder because of medium lock-in (Crash reports exportable). Plan for a dual-run period so you can validate parity before decommissioning.

On our developer-experience score, Sentry is rated 5/5 and Backtrace 4/5, so Sentry edges ahead. Scores like this smooth over real differences in docs, CLI feel, and SDK quality, so treat them as a starting point. Spend an afternoon in each before relying on the number.

faq.q: Is Sentry a good alternative to Backtrace?
faq.a: Sentry is a reasonable alternative to Backtrace when Sentry fits error tracking and crash reporting for any platform. If your use case matches Backtrace's sweet spot — mobile — staying with Backtrace is probably safer. Backtrace itself is not meant for pre-launch, Simple projects, which is often where users start shopping for alternatives.
